item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for cute fungus thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not just bird.

But super bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lord Graywing is really bird. So he can fly. With bird powers, because he's super bird.

Oh my goodness yes. +1

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. The "box" does not necessarily mean a literal box (as in a locker, containment cell, etc.) The "box" is the containment procedure, and as long as the "box" isn't too fiddly, it's Safe. Feeding and cleaning up after them is part of the "box". They aren't a separate procedure needed to keep the "box" working effectively.

Yes, that's the box here. However Safe objects are supposed to be fine if you lock them in a box and leave them alone indefinitely. That second part doesn't seem to apply to this particular box.

One thing that object classes could be for is a way to categorically organize broad general policies. This would prevent the Foundation from wasting time and resources with a special case for everything, or worse, failing because trying to consistently apply thousands of specific policies is a recipe for disaster. I like a stricter intrepation of object classes for this reason.

+ What do you mean?
– hide block


If all safe objects are inertly sitting in hard vaults, all Euclid objects require ongoing but routine maintenance, and all Keter objects require high priority, difficult, or dynamic maintenance, then the Foundation can:

  • Have $x/object budgeted for Safe containment based on the average locker in a vault, $nx/object dedicated to Euclid containment based on the average cost of a containment cell and for care and feeding, and object specific budgets for Keter containment plus a pessimistic estimate of what unknown new Keters might cost.
  • Have general policies for things like lockdowns, evacuation, and abandon-in-place. Being able to say that you can just lock up all the Safe objects is really useful here, and doesn't work if some Safes need to be evacuated or they'll die. Needing to reference and apply hundreds of special case instructions for every object at a facility is a terrible plan for an emergency.
  • Be able to design sites with future needs in mind and organize containment efficiently. Having some big vaults (for Safes) and cell blocks (for Euclids) behind multiple outer security layers is much better than having a random mix of different containment systems.

That said there really isn't any consensus about this, and different authors do things differently. The only thing that matters is that more people like an article than hate it. I completely disagree with this being Safe, but not enough to downvote an otherwise great article, especially for what's essentially a procedural quibble about a wholly fictional bureaucratic process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really digging the "Cadaververse" you've created.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've written an article that is literally "Keter-class because it teleports around and makes people crazy" and I feel personally attacked by this statement :P

In all seriousness, I agree 100% with this sentiment. The imagery and characterisation work beautifully together here, and the whole thing was just an absolute pleasure to read.

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded:
I see the logic here - you could define "box" a number of ways - but an enclosed orchard would require maintenance (even if the thing takes care of the plants itself, you'd need someone to keep an eye on it to make sure that stays stable) and eventual repairs for whatever irrigation system you have in there. Feeding it waste would require someone to gather and deliver the waste.

Even though it doesn't want to escape, it seems pretty sociable, and there's no reason to think it would react to solitary confinement any better than any other intelligent being does. So you would need to provide it with companions and probably the occasional evaluation to make sure that its mental condition is stable. Since its body seems to be composed of a pretty complex ecosystem, you would probably also want some kind of veterinarian or zoologist to take a look at him from time to time.

So now we've got a botanist, a couple different kinds of engineer, a zoologist, maintenance workers, and counselors all involved in the containment of this thing. Those personnel need to be scheduled, managed, transported etc. This is a box that would require a good deal of ongoing intervention to maintain, which is the definition of Euclid. It's not just about the difficulty of preventing a breach, it's about the difficulty of keeping the thing secure and protected in addition to containing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's rare that there's a Safe level intelligent anomaly that actually makes sense to be so. And you absolutely nailed it there. Beyond that, it's nice to see something that's not "Keter-class because it teleports around and makes people crazy if you don't hit them with the Forget Me Stick." There are so few new SCPs that are this endearing. So, +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. The "box" does not necessarily mean a literal box (as in a locker, containment cell, etc.) The "box" is the containment procedure, and as long as the "box" isn't too fiddly, it's Safe. Feeding and cleaning up after them is part of the "box". They aren't a separate procedure needed to keep the "box" working effectively.

This one's "box" is literally "leave it in an enclosed orchard" and "feed it anything hazardous or compostable."

According to the style guidelines:

any SCP that's autonomous, sentient and/or sapient is generally classified as Euclid, due to the inherent unpredictability of an object that can act or think on its own.

However, I am inclined to say this could be classified as Safe. Despite being intelligent, it doesn't want to escape. It perfectly happy where it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This "mother" entity is already extremely interesting to me. I like the way you connected this to Greywing, scp-3893, and the rest of those malevolent entities surrounding the sorrow.

Also not to mention it's adorable
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3982: Oh my goodness yes.

Aaaand upvote.

And yet the interview just kept getting better toward the end. I don't know what this is, but I know I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A corpse eating trash has no right being this cute.

+1

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded: I've never understood the locked box metaphor to be what defines object classes, it's just an easy way to teach someone basically what they mean (in particular, that it's not a representation of how dangerous the item is).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dialogue in this is fantastic! I love the way that 3982 talks; it manages to be both coherent and incoherent at the same time. And also very, very cute. +1

item.title: More lore (and unnecessary questions)!
item.content:encoded:
This thing sounds like the perfect anti-3885 entity.

So, is this a "technology vs nature" dichotomy? From 3982s rambling, it seems like she (I know you didn't give her a gender, I just thinks she sounds like a little girl) was "sent" up here to get rid of pollution and/or dead things. Which all seems to be what 3885 and 3983 are all about. Who's the aggressor here?

Are Graywing and "the baron" subservient to Mother?

Is the baron the Boss (and therefore not subservient to Mother, but I digress)?

What is the Sorrow doing during all of this? (I get that this is set during/before the Mother crisis, but if these are all in the same universe, then they all should be reacting to each other in some way)

So the Glade is down. Is the Glade hell? What was the "bad light"? (I'm pulling at straws here, I know)

Who was the "super good man" that 3982 was eating?

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded: It sounds like this still needs to be fed, cleaned up after, interacted with etc. like most living things. Since it needs to be cared for, it wouldn't really be secured/protected by being put in a locked box forever, which is the classic definition of Safe. This is why so many authors have a "living things are automatically Euclid" headcanon.

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded: E: Haydenhead layed it on thicker than I did.

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded: That isn't strictly true. A sapient entity with a long history of total conformity and respect for its for own detainment, such as this one, doesn't need to be Euclid at all.

item.title: Object class
item.content:encoded: Nice, fairly interesting scip, but with sapient entities you should always give them object class euclid. Just a heads up.

item.title: *leafy hugs*
item.content:encoded:
my fifteenth thing. a squishy plant fellow who is quite friendly.

thanks again to the illustrious Weryllium for providing clearance.

and thank you, reader, for being there. ♥
